skill.name: dbt-transformation-patterns
skill.description: Apply dbt transformation patterns for analytics engineering projects, including model layers, tests, documentation, macros, incremental models, and project conventions.
skill.summary: Build organized dbt projects with tested models, documented sources, and practical incremental strategies.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high and medium patterns, but review found Markdown documentation, dbt SQL/Jinja examples, and public reference links. No executable scripts, prompt injection attempt, credential access, malware behavior, or data exfiltration pattern was found in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fence and dbt Examples
low_findings.description: The external command detections are caused by Markdown code fences, dbt CLI examples, SQL, YAML, and Jinja snippets. They are instructional text and are not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is plain Markdown documentation and examples. There is no script file, runtime hook, shell interpolation, or user input execution path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Matches in Documentation
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections map to ordinary dbt source, model, column, and documentation text. No weak hash algorithm, encryption routine, credential handling, or cryptographic operation appears at the cited locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found descriptions, column names, and testing examples only. No cryptographic API or algorithm name is used in an executable context.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Tokens in SQL Examples
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance detections are common database terms such as id fields, relationships, and model joins inside dbt examples. They do not collect host, network, process, or environment information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matching lines are dbt schema tests and SQL joins. They do not invoke operating system discovery commands or access machine metadata.

low_findings.title: Public Documentation Links Only
low_findings.description: The network detections are static links to dbt documentation, best practices, package documentation, and forum resources. The skill does not fetch these links or transmit data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible Markdown references to public dbt resources. No network client, webhook, credential upload, or hidden endpoint is present.

content.value_statement: Analytics teams need consistent dbt patterns to keep models understandable and reliable. This skill gives practical structures for layers, tests, documentation, macros, and incremental processing.
actual_capabilities: Defines staging, intermediate, and mart model layer patterns.
Provides dbt source, model, and column documentation examples.
Shows testing patterns for uniqueness, null checks, relationships, recency, and accepted values.
Explains incremental model configuration options such as merge, delete and insert, and insert overwrite.
Includes macro examples for reusable transformations and development filters.
Lists common dbt commands for build, test, docs, debug, and selection workflows.
limitations: Does not inspect a live warehouse or validate real schemas automatically.
Does not include adapter-specific tuning for every data warehouse.
Requires users to adapt model names, sources, and tests to their own project.
Provides guidance and examples, not a complete generated dbt project.

use_cases.title: Standardize a New dbt Project
use_cases.description: Create a clear project layout with staging, intermediate, and mart layers before model development grows.

use_cases.title: Improve Data Quality Coverage
use_cases.description: Add practical tests, source freshness checks, and column documentation to important models.

use_cases.title: Plan Incremental Transformations
use_cases.description: Choose incremental strategies and refresh patterns for large tables that cannot be rebuilt on every run.

prompt_templates.title: Organize My dbt Models
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the dbt transformation patterns skill to propose a staging, intermediate, and mart folder structure for my project. My sources are [sources], and my main business entities are [entities].

prompt_templates.title: Add Tests and Documentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to recommend dbt tests and documentation for these models: [model list]. Include source freshness, key constraints, relationships, and accepted values where appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Incremental Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design an incremental dbt model for [table name]. The unique key is [key], late arriving data can appear after [window], and the warehouse is [warehouse].

prompt_templates.title: Review a dbt Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the dbt transformation patterns skill to review this dbt architecture: [project summary]. Identify layering issues, test gaps, documentation gaps, macro opportunities, and incremental strategy risks.

output_examples.input: I have Stripe payments and Shopify orders. Help me structure the dbt project.
output_examples.output: A recommended layout separates raw source definitions, staging models for Stripe and Shopify, intermediate finance models, and mart models for customers, orders, and revenue.

output_examples.input: My orders fact table is slow to rebuild.
output_examples.output: The response would compare merge, delete and insert, and partition overwrite strategies, then recommend keys, lookback windows, and tests for the orders model.
The response would list model-level and column-level tests for primary keys, required fields, relationships, freshness, accepted values, and metric sanity checks.

best_practices: Keep raw source cleanup in staging models so downstream logic stays consistent.
Document models and columns when you add tests, not after the project is complete.
Use incremental models only when keys, update timestamps, and late data behavior are understood.
anti_patterns: Building marts directly from raw sources without a staging layer.
Using incremental materialization without a tested unique key or refresh plan.
Copying business logic across models instead of moving shared logic into macros or intermediate models.

faq.question: Does this skill create a complete dbt project?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns, examples, and review guidance that users adapt to their project.

faq.question: Can it help with dbt tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers common tests for keys, nulls, relationships, accepted values, freshness, and recency.

faq.question: Does it support incremental models?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains common incremental strategies and when each strategy is useful.

faq.question: Does it run dbt commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It lists useful commands as documentation, but the skill itself does not execute them.

faq.question: Is this useful for non-dbt SQL projects?
faq.answer: Only partly. The guidance is designed for dbt projects with warehouse-backed transformations.

faq.question: Can it replace warehouse-specific performance tuning?
faq.answer: No. Users still need adapter and warehouse knowledge for partitioning, clustering, and cost tuning.
